administrative_details.name_of_data_source: Clinical Practice Research Datalink AURUM and Hospital Episode Statistics
administrative_details.main_references: Wolf A, Dedman D,Campbell J,Booth H,Lunn D,Chapman J,Myles P "Data resource profile: Clinical Practice Research Datalink (CPRD) Aurum." International journal of epidemiology (2019): 30859197

data_elements_collected.population: The CPRD Aurum database includes around 25.5% of the UK population. All currently contributing practices are in England, but coverage varies between English regions. The Aurum OMOP CDM database covers the period from 01/01/1995 to the most recent data collection ). For the Aurum + linked HES APC database, the coverage period is truncated to ensure that all patients are under observation in both the primary care and linked HES data.
data_elements_collected.healthcare_setting_type_of_data:
Primary care – General Practitioner, and secondary care – specialists (ambulatory or hospital outpatient care), and hospital inpatient care. 

CPRD Aurum includes data captured in UK primary care. In the UK, almost all individuals are registered with a single general practitioner (GP), and around 90% of all healthcare contacts with the UK National Health Service (NHS) occur in primary care (also known as general practice). GPs have a central role as gatekeepers for access to secondary and tertiary services, and other community based services and intervention programmes. Widespread adoption of dedicated computer software in UK primary care began in the late 1980s and was almost universal by 1995. GPs and general practice staff use these software to manage patients' electronic health records which include registration and demographic information, symptoms, diagnoses, clinical investigations and laboratory tests, prescriptions for medicines and appliances, a wide range of behavioural and social factors relevant to health and wellbeing, and administrative information. Some information is captured on contacts with other health services, including referral details and discharge summaries and other communications from those services. CPRD Aurum does not include any associated free text notes, scanned documents or images from the patient record. Hospital Episode Statistics Admitted Patient Care (HES APC) data captures information on inpatient care delivered in NHS hospitals and independent treatment centres funded by the NHS. Key variables include age; sex; ethnicity, episode start/end date; a single primary diagnosis (ICD-10), with up to 19 other comorbidity diagnoses; up to 24 procedures and other interventions (with date), coded using the UK Office of Population Censuses and Surveys Classification of Interventions and Procedures classification (OPCS-4). Medical treatments (drugs and devices) and lab values are not recorded in HES APC.
data_elements_collected.data_collection_process: Outpatient electronic health records, and Inpatient hospital electronic health records.                                                                                                                                                                                                        Patient EHR data are entered by practice staff using the EMIS Web GP practice management software. Data may also be captured automatically via electronic links from pathology and diagnostic laboratories. EMIS Web is a cloud based system, and practice data are uploaded to the vendor data centre. Pseudonymised data is extracted from the vendor system and downloaded to CPRD via a dedicated cloud based interface. CPRD undertake additional checks to ensure data conform to specification, and comply with established data quality metrics, and apply additional pseudomymisation steps. HES APC data are derived from hospital activity data which are abstracted from clinical records and discharge summaries by trained clinical coders according to centrally issued coding rules and standards.  The data is collected centrally for monitoring, planning, and payment purposes. For secondary uses including research, additional cleaning and curation is undertaken to generate the HES APC data. This includes linking episodes for the same individual, and addition of a Master Person Service identifier (MPS ID) which allows linkage to primary care and other health datasets for research.
data_elements_collected.general_representativeness: CPRD Aurum provides a purposive sample of English general practices using EMIS Web GP software. EMIS Web has an uneven regional coverage. Participation in the CPRD data collection is voluntary. While not a random sample, CPRD Aurum is broadly representative of the overall population in terms of age and gender (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30859197/), with minor differences in terms of area based deprivation and urban-rural distribution. (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35902219/)
data_elements_collected.data_source_coding: Data is captured using READ, EMIS2, SNOMED, DM+D, ICD10 and OPSC4 codes.
data_elements_collected.source_quality_control: CPRD follows the same three-staged processing pipeline for all the data that it curates: curate individual data collections; integrate data into master database; and extract study specific datasets. For each part of the processing pipeline, CPRD applies checks to ensure that the quality of the data is maintained.   At the data curation stage, conducted at both a collection level and at a record level, the data is verified to ensure that the data conforms to an agreed specification. Collection level verification ensures that the collection contains only expected data files, that the collection files are from a known source, and that the collections’ files are in the correct sequence.  The record level verifications performed at Level 1 are structural in nature and are applied to every data element that CPRD receives without considering the context of the data item against the entirety of the master database.  These checks include that the data item is of the correct type, that fields that should have values do indeed contain a value, and that, where specified, the format and length of the data is accurate. Any violations of these checks result in the collection being rejected, the data not being integrated into the master database, and further inspection or recollection of the data taking place.  At the data integration stage, the actual content of the data is validated, considering any existing content already collected.  These validations are contextual in nature and consider the values of multiple data items within the master database.  As CPRD provide real world data no data is modified before being made available for research. The data is either rejected (as per the data curation checks) or integrated.  Once integrated, data is not corrected, but patient, practice, and database level data quality metrics and markers are put in place, based on internal consistency of the data, to aid researchers in deciding if the data is suitable for research.  There are some exceptions to this no modification principle.  In the case that a patient is validated as being opted out, then their data is removed from future versions of the database to ensure their wishes are respected. Further validation and cleaning of study specific datasets may be undertaken by researchers or as part of good data management, and is dependent upon the research question and the researchers performing the analysis.
data_elements_collected.linkage: Linkage of CPRD Aurum to HES APC is undertaken by NHS England. Identifiers for patients in CPRD Aurum (NHS number, gender, full date of birth, residential postcode) flow directly from the vendor data centre to NHS England, and are linked to HES APC via the MPS ID. A bridging file of patient index numbers for each dataset is returned to CPRD to allow linkage of the clinical data. Linkage is undertaken at approximately 6 month intervals, but this may be impacted by organisation factors external to CPRD. Linkage to additional datasets is expected over the next 12-24 months, including linked death registrations (for accurate cause of death), hospital outpatient activity, and cancer registrations.
data_elements_collected.mortality: The great majority of deaths are eventually captured in patients’ primary care record. Published comparisons found that over 98% of national death registrations (the gold standard) were also captured in the CPRD GOLD primary care database https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30908785/, and results are similar for CPRD Aurum (unpublished). Estimated date of death in the primary care record shows good agreement with registered date of death (within 30 days in 98.8% of occurrences after 2013), but cause of death is captured inconsistently.
data_elements_collected.source_limitations: CPRD Aurum uses data collected for the primary purpose of managing and delivering safe and effective patient care in general practice. There are requirements and incentives for practice staff to record high quality information, but there is heterogeneity between practices and over time. UK primary care databases have been extensively validated and found to be of sufficient quality to support observational research. CPRD Aurum captures information on prescriptions issued by practice staff, from which drug exposures can only be inferred. There is no information about drug exposures in secondary care via the HES APC.

omop_standardisation.omop_mapping: The data has been mapped to the OMOP CDM v5 and the OMOP standard vocabularies.
omop_standardisation.omop_quality_control: The format, structural and semantic conformance has been verified upon onboarding into the DARWIN EU® data network. Patients can move and receive a different ID number upon changing practice, but a patient can only be registered at one practice at a time.  Upon registering at a new practice, a patient’s records will be transferred with them, and they will be deregistered at their previous practice.  CPRD cannot track patients across practices as the data is de-identified and a patient key is generated for each patient at a practice level, not a country level.  The patient key will remain the same for as long as the patient is registered at the practice. The main impact of patients changing practice is to reduce the average follow up time available for each analytical unit (i.e. patient-within-practice). A secondary issue is that records for the same individual split across two or more practices are not strictly independent. The impact on inferential statistics is likely to be very limited, and analytical adjustment is typically not required (or applied).
